title: WRITTEN IN WATER
text: WRITTEN IN WATER by Robert Ford I ’m sitting, crisscross-applesauce, in the deepest part of the deepest end of the Twin Oaks Community Center swimming pool, but down here, I may as well be sitting on Mars. My eyes are closed, have been closed, for about five minutes. I’ve never been able to open my eyes underwater. It makes me uncomfortable. It’s not the pressure against them, it’s that everything is blurry and out of focus. It’s like I’m viewing the world as I’m dying, as an almost-ghost, caught between the living and the dead. It makes me uncomfortable, and so I keep my eyes closed. I sit very still in the darkness, letting my limp arms drift with any current the water might carry. I wait for my twin-sister Della to reach from the shadows and touch my shoulder. We do this thing that bonds us together so tightly. Della keeps me alive. Literally. I take a deep breath and swim to the previously mentioned deepest of deeps, sit down like a Yogi Mystic, and slowly let the air seep out from between my lips. Sometimes the bubbles catch my nostrils on the way up and tickle me, but I keep myself from laughing. I can feel Della doing her best mermaid impersonation. I can feel the rush of water as she kicks, and I imagine she’s a real mermaid. It makes me smile, even though no one will see my expression. When my lungs are almost empty, I stop feeling Della swimming around me, but a moment later, right as I start to burn inside, I feel her hand softly land on my left shoulder, and then her lips are on mine, gently pushing the air from her lungs into me. After a moment, she swims back to the surface, takes another breath, and comes down to do it again. Over and over. I could be down here for an hour, kept alive from this connection with my sister. It’s an unbelievably powerful feeling. I miss Della. I miss her so much. When she died, I saw her lying in that hospital bed and I wanted to put my lips on hers and breathe air into her lungs to bring her back to life, but my father stopped me. He said it wasn’t the right thing to do, that it wouldn’t help, but I still wonder. # I’m in Mrs. Eckenrode’s Advanced Biology class. She uses an erasable marker on the whiteboard at the front of the room, and draws the parts of a mushroom. It looks like a UFO to me, or a distorted version of the worksheets we were given in Sex-Ed. That thought makes me stifle a giggle and, from across the room, I can feel Della turn toward me. We’re never allowed to sit together in any of our classes. Her expression is amused, and I wonder, as I often do, if we occasionally have the same thoughts at the same time. She raises an eyebrow questioningly and I glance at the front of the room to make sure Mrs. Eckenrode’s back is still to us. It is, and I point to the drawing of the UFO/penis on the whiteboard. I look back at Della, make a fist with my right hand, and get ready to push my tongue into my left cheek to the internationally recognized mimic for blowjob. But then I see her face has changed in the split-second I looked away. Della appears absolutely and completely drained of color. The hint of humor on her face is replaced by an expression of desperation. I know what she’s going to do before she does. At least, I think I so. Della’s right hand covers her stomach. she hunches over and throws up beside Lori Wilson’s new backpack with Post Malone’s face on it. This is the start of it. # Della is… gifted . She excels in volleyball and almost every single class in school. But excel isn’t quite right. To excel in something, you’d have to try . Della doesn’t. It’s effortless to her. She is naturally good at everything. One time at the Twin Oaks ping pong table, I watched her beat a guy, six years older, in a best out of five match. She found out later the guy had been state champion for two years in a row, and before that, Della had played exactly two games of ping pong in her entire life, one of which had been with me about a half hour earlier. I’m not jealous. I’m really not. Sure, there are times I wish I was right up there with her, but that’s okay. Della doesn’t take it for granted. She knows it. There’s no way not to know, really, but she doesn’t flaunt it. The thing about Della is she’s genuinely nice without being sickeningly so. One time, in the second grade, I threw up. It was on the school playground, and I’d been playing on the monkey bars way too long. The end of recess bell went off and I walked about fifteen feet before I turned and hurled my cafeteria pizza in a row of marigolds. That was over eight years ago and people have never forgotten. When Della throws up, there’s a beat of absolute silence, and Lori seems to realize how close Post Malone’s face has come to getting covered with vomit. She wrinkles up her nose and uses her sneakered foot to slide her bag to the front of her desk. But then, I watch her slowly realize it was Della who threw up, and this pure and completely sincere sympathy washes over Lori’s face. She stands up and puts her hand on Della’s shoulder, and over the next few moments, ends up getting told by Mrs. Eckenrode to personally escort Della to the School Nurse. This is unusual and makes me feel uneasy. Nothing bad really ever happens to Della. Like I said, she’s gifted. Della’s good luck is really annoying to me, and a little tougher to accept, only if because I seem to have a black cloud over me at times. If there’s something to stub my toe on, I’ll find it. I am a splinter magnet. I will spill any liquid in a glass as efficiently as oil company tankers, washing over homework or art projects, important papers, you name it. The day Della throws up in class, it’s like the well of good luck she’s been storing has suddenly run dry. Somehow, karma has been keeping track of the absence of bad things in her life, until it makes up for it, all at once. # I see Mom at the kitchen counter, wiping something down with a sponge. She glances at me over her shoulder, her cleaning the important thing here. “Della’s resting so don’t play your music.” I use one foot and then the other to take my shoes off on the little rug in the front hall. I lean down and put my sneakers next to Della’s. “Did she throw up again?” Mom doesn’t bother looking, intent on the circular motions of cleaning the counter. “Once, right after she got home.” I don’t say anything in response, and walk the narrow staircase upstairs to our shared bedroom. My parents had us when they were young. She was nineteen, and he was twenty. Mom quit college one week into her first semester, and Dad worked night shift while he was going to college during the day. She worked as a waitress at a diner right up until her water broke during a lunch shift. I’m sure it was tough. It’s crazy to think of being twenty years old, a new parent, and have college and a job and responsibilities. I get it. But their failure to use birth control isn’t our fault. So anyway, we share a bedroom. Dad has been talking about moving to a bigger house for as long as I can remember. But his words and any actual action have been at war for years. Almost every time the news is on, he’ll mumble something along the lines of “Economy’s in the dumps. Rich are gettin’ richer every damned day, and the workin’ man ends up screwed.” Truth is, Della and I don’t mind sharing a room. We never have. I figure it has something to do with being in a womb together for nine months. If we can get along in a tiny house like that, then sharing a bedroom is a cake walk. Della is lying on her bed when I creep into the room. She’s clutching her pillow, and her legs are curled up. I stare at her for a moment, wondering if that’s how she slept inside Mom. Even though I haven’t made a sound, her eyes flutter open and she turns her head slightly to look at me. Della offers me a crooked smile that fades as quickly as it arrived, a moment of sunshine poking through a cloudy sky. “How ya feelin’?” I step closer, see the damp strands of hair pasted to her pale, sweat-beaded forehead. On the nightstand between our beds, there’s an unfolded washcloth sitting on a plastic Zip-Lock baggie. I haven’t even set my backpack down, but I use the damp washcloth to gently wipe Della’s forehead, smoothing the hair away from her face. Her eyes close again, and I listen to the soft rhythm of how she breathes when she’s sleeping. Even so, I hold off on homework and read Charlotte’s Web to her until Mom calls me for dinner. It’s always been Della’s favorite book. # It’s the third Saturday of the month and we’re all supposed to go to Gramma’s house for the monthly pilgrimage. Della was awake twice during the night, which means I was too. Over the last few weeks, I’ve turned into a human tripwire, on alert most of the night, listening for the splash of vomit in the now-permanent fixture of a bucket by Della’s bed. Her eyes are closed when I get up, and while she’s sleeping, I take a good look at her. When Mrs. Eckenrode was done drawing deformed penises on the white board, she talked about mushroom spores and how they flourish . Whatever the opposite of flourish is, Della is exactly that. Her skin, somehow always blemish-free and the shade of highly-creamed coffee, is now the color of the pages in an old paperback novel. Beneath her eyes, there are darkened shapes of crescent moons turned on their side. Even her hair has changed, the thick waves shrunken somehow to thin strands that cling together. I want to mess with her. Stick the tip of my index finger up one of her nostrils, or run my fingertips, spiderweb softly, against her ear. I want to mess with her and watch her open her eyes and swat my hand away as she giggles herself awake. But I don’t. I put on jeans and a shirt my parents will say is acceptable for a visit to Gramma’s house. Della doesn’t bat an eyelid as I finish dressing, and I stop and stare at her again. When I leave, and softly shut the door behind me, I feel like I’m leaving her alone at the bottom of the pool at Twin Oaks. # Mom stays with Della, and it’s only Dad and I on the drive. I stay off my phone as he doesn’t like when I’m on it and it’s only the two of us. Says he feels like he’s being ignored . Not that it matters much today, as Dad’s humming along to the music on a classic rock station and not talking to me anyway. As he drives, I study his facial expressions from the corner of my eye. He looks distracted. I wonder what he’s thinking about because every once in a while, his eyebrows knit together like he’s remembering something painful. Mom’s been wearing the same kind of expression lately, which is unusual. It stands out because my mother is normally what you’d call nondescript. She doesn’t stand out at all. Ever. That sounds harsh until you meet her. Her style is no style. I’ve been wondering what she’s been thinking too, but I’m not going to ask. Not her, or my dad. No one asks your parents that kind of thing. I know they love us. I mean, obviously. Dad’s still taking me to Gramma’s house to visit, and Mom is taking care of Della back home. Of course , they love us. There are moments when I’m just not sure either one of them likes us much. # Because she asked me to, I sit on Della’s bed and try to help her with Applied Economics homework. I hear her sigh in frustration, plop her head down on her pillow. Both of us know this isn’t gonna happen right now. “It’s okay.” Della doesn’t reply and when I see her eyes are glassy, borderline crying, I don’t say anything either. Right now, I’m not sure if there’s anything I can say to help. She’s not getting better. I glance at the prescription bottles on the nightstand. I want to scream at them for not doing their job, but I reach out and give Della’s shoulder a soft squeeze. I reach into my backpack and pull out a small stack of Get Well Soon cards from people at school. I can’t help but notice the amount is getting fewer from only a couple weeks ago, and when I put the pile on the nightstand by the pill bottles, I see Della look at them. I can tell she notices, too. Della ignores the cards and so I lay down beside her on my back and start watching stupid videos on my phone. After a few minutes, she rolls over and joins me. It feels good, her being beside me like this, and though she doesn’t laugh, I hear her snicker several times. For right now, that’s enough. Every once in a while, I find myself staring at her for too long, studying her as if she was some sort of sculpture. She catches me sometimes, but Della never says anything. I see this quick expression drift over her face, and then she nestles down onto the bed and pulls the quilt up close. On the nights she catches me staring, I leave my own bed and lay beside her. Part of me knows how bad this really is. Part of me is trying to memorize her. I want to wrap her up and save her. I dream on those nights. Always, the same dream of being in the deep end of the pool and how it feels sitting there with all that water around me, waiting for my sister to return from the surface, the land of the living, and breathe life into my lungs. In my dreams, she swims away and never comes back down. I always wake up sweating, clawing at the empty air above my bed. # I am a mixture of anger and disgust as I march upstairs. Right after school dismissal, Gavin Bowers asked me to go with him to the Spring Dance. Admittedly, him talking to me made the nest of tummy snakes inside me coil up into a tight writhing ball. Gavin is, undisputed by everyone, the cutest boy in high school. Not in tenth grade, but in the entire student body . This is the first time I’ve ever been asked out, and it’s Gavin Bowers. Any girl would be absolutely stupid over his invitation. Except I can’t allow myself to feel that way, or say yes, or even smile at his words, because Della liked Gavin Bowers first, and she likes him. A lot. But as far as I know, Gavin has never even sent a card to Della since she’s been out of school. That should say everything that needs said. That first fleeting elation in my gut is chased away by an unshakable feeling of Ewwww. He can’t get my sister so he’s trying his luck with me , the cheaper, knockoff copy. Right there in front of everyone, waiting for the school buses to line up, I start yelling at him. It comes out so quickly, I’m not sure what I say, but it cuts him to his core, because he slinks away into the crowd. A teacher I don’t know stares at me, but doesn’t say anything. Word must have spread among the staff, and I guess when your sister is dying, you get away with a lot. I’m still full of rage as I reach the top of the stairs and walk into our bedroom, ready to spew about what an asshole Gavin is, but Mom’s there, sitting on Della’s bed and spoon-feeding her chicken soup. My anger evaporates. I put my backpack on the floor, lie down on my bed, and watch them. “Pepperoni pizza for dinner tonight.” Mom’s voice is fake enthusiasm. I groan inside. It’ll be the third time in the last week we’ve gotten pizza from Sal’s. Someone baked us a deep-dish lasagna a couple days ago, and I’ve had enough red sauce to last me a while, but I keep quiet. Della’s eyes are barely open, but she keeps opening her mouth and accepting food like a drowsy baby bird. Mom’s quick hand follows up after each spoonful, dabbing a napkin on Della’s chin. I hear the spoon scraping the bowl and watch Mom deliver the last of it. She sighs and stands and glances at me before she leaves the room. Her footsteps sound abnormally heavy as she descends downstairs. I watch Della’s sleepy eyes, the hint of a smile as her gaze finds me, and then it grows smaller and distant, like bubbles rising to the surface of water. I’m not going to tell Della about Gavin. I realize there’s no point. # Della has sent me on a top-secret mission. I take this request very seriously, though given the circumstances, I don’t think our mother would complain much about taking one of her special chocolate truffles for Della. I’ve already made up my mind to steal two of them instead of the one Della asked me to get for her. She needs something to smile about. I hear my mother talking on the phone, and as I reach the bottom of the steps, I see the shadows of movement in the living room. “They just don’t know, Janice. Test after goddamned test, and I… Oh, I know, I know. Making money out the ass and can’t figure out what’s wrong.” Her voice drops in tone, worn and weary, and for a split second, I almost feel guilty taking the chocolate truffles, but it’s only for a split second. I make my way into the kitchen and pause in front of the refrigerator. In my mind, red laser beams crisscross in front of me in a deadly crosshatch pattern, daring me to try to get past them. I take a deep breath, exhale slowly, and blindly feel around on top of the fridge until I touch the round tin hidden up there. I drag it closer, bring it down with both hands, and pop the lid off. Inside, I see a variety of spheres wrapped in beautiful red, blue, and golden foil. I take two of the gold ones, pause to wonder if Mom counts the number of colors, and decide to take one gold and one blue. I slide the tin back on top of the fridge, careful not to touch one of the red laser beams and trigger the alarm. Satisfied with the placement, I pocket the truffles and turn, put my back against the wall, and inch toward the stairs again. I make my way to the doorframe, and Mom walks by me quickly, the phone still pressed against her ear. She doesn’t notice me at all. “I don’t know, Janice. I truly don’t, but thank you.” She goes to the kitchen sink, pulls a cooking pot from the drying rack, and then takes a can of chicken noodle soup from the corner cabinet. “No, no. The lasagna was great, but soup is just about all she can keep down anymore.” Mom opens the can of soup and dumps it into the pot. She adds some tap water to the mixture and sets the pot on the counter. “Hang on, Janice.” She puts the phone down and opens the cabinets beneath the sink. I watch her take out a small blue bottle, but her back is to me and though I don’t see what she does with it at the counter, she bends down and puts the bottle away again. Pressed tightly against the wall, my spy fantasy dissolves. Mom puts the pot of soup on the stove, and I shift around the corner and sneak upstairs. The smile on Della’s face when she savors the first truffle is something to behold. # The air in the funeral home feels artificially moist. It’s like mist is getting piped in from some kind of hidden machine that hasn’t been cleaned in a long time and smells like rainwater in an old tire. Flower bouquets bookend the gray casket at the front of the room. Half of its silk-lined lid is opened up, but I haven’t approached it yet. A few people speak at the service. Mrs. Eckenrode makes a brief speech about how bright and talented Della was. Gramma sits in the front row and dabs at her eyes with a crumpled tissue. A pastor speaks about rose gardens in heaven, but I tune out the rest of whatever he says. Mom is the last to take the podium, and I have to tune out whatever she’s saying. Her eyes scan the audience, pause on certain people as she speaks. I stare at my lap and let her words become an insect’s drone. Afterward, I see people approach Mom and hug her tightly. I see them nodding, but can’t hear the exchange. I realize I don’t care. The crowd is thinning and I know what I have to do. There’s not a lot of time left, but even so, my mind still teeter-totters on the action. I find myself moving toward the open casket, as if my mind’s already been made up but didn’t bother to tell me. Della wears the dark blue dress she got for Homecoming last year. It’s not exactly boring, but it’s not exciting either. She wears a thin golden necklace with a crucifix, and it makes me roll my eyes. None of us have stepped foot in a church or sang a hymn in over ten years. Even through the layers of make-up someone has caked on her face, I want to describe Della’s expression as peaceful . But peaceful isn’t quite right. I lean down and put my hand against Della’s left cheek. Her skin is cool and feels too smooth and waxy, like a Crayon peeled of its wrapper. With her eyes closed, she looks like I probably do at the bottom of the pool. I move closer, leaning down, and I feel someone stepping beside me, so I straighten up again. Dad doesn’t make eye contact, but his lids are red and raw. All night, he’s been carrying around the same glass with ice and some kind of liquor in it. I’m not sure if he keeps refilling or if he’s simply not drinking it. I know he knows I’m looking at him, but he still doesn’t catch my eyes. His face scrunches up and this high-pitched yip noise erupts from his chest. He shakes his head, clears his throat harshly, and then turns and walks away without a word. I watch as other men speak to him in low voices, nod at his sad face, and clap him on the back or give him bro hugs, even the act of comforting grief is too manly to be tainted by the close contact of a full embrace. I turn back to Della and stare. This is not my sister. This is nothing but a cheap knockoff copy. # I notice the way people look at me now in school. I never used to pay attention, but I see expressions of sympathy on people’s faces. Some are sincere, and some I can tell are sheep going along with the crowd, doing what they think they’re supposed to be doing. But both of the groups make a sad face when they see me and offer the same flat-mouthed chagrin. Then there’s the other people I catch looking. Their eyes tell a story other than sympathy. They give a glance and shift away quickly. Most of them suddenly seem to have forgotten something in their last class. They get away from me as fast as they can, as if they’re scared to catch whatever I have, and one of their siblings will end up dying, too. I have a fleeting feeling I hate all of them, but to be honest, I don’t really feel much at all. I’m half of a void, and I’m reminded of it every day. Della’s empty chair in the classroom. Her empty seat on the school bus. Her now empty, untouched locker. A pair of Della’s sneakers remain in the downstairs hallway for a month after her funeral. One day, Dad came inside and stopped, looked down and stared for a moment, and then walked right back outside and stayed in the garage for a long while. # With Della gone, the house became the empty socket of a pulled tooth. Something used to be there, but it’s gone now, and the only thing you do in your search is probe the spot where it used to be over and over again. Of course, it’s pointless, but that doesn’t prevent you from doing it. At least that’s what the house has become to me . Mom started having friends over. Card game every Thursday afternoon, complete with chardonnay and snack trays. When they see me get home from school and I walk past the opening to the dining room, they smile and go quiet until I reach the top of the stairs. Then they speak in voices too low for me to make out what they’re saying. It’s the tone of voice people talk in when it’s something bad. Part of me wonders if they’re asking Mom how I’m doing, but if I dig a little deeper past that feeling, I find I don’t really care much about that either. Dad is here, but not here. This has changed him, and he’s like a not-quite-in-tune radio station. There’s static all the time, and on some days, there’s more noise than others. I want to grab my father and find the stereo knob I need to adjust and get a clear signal again. But I leave him be. I give a polite if insincere smile to the soccer moms sipping their white wine, and I let Mom do her thing. I go upstairs to our room (yes, I still call it that), and lay down on my bed and my mind explores that empty tooth socket. # It’s Saturday morning and my body isn’t sure how to deal with the first big breakfast Mom has made in almost two months. Orange juice and bacon and scrambled eggs and buttered toast all sit in my stomach like a dysfunctional family. I’m walking off the urge to crawl back into bed and go to sleep. I walk past Browning Street, and keep going until I reach Twin Oaks, where I stop and stare at the entrance, wondering if anyone’s up for a game of air hockey, but my heart’s not in it. I stuff my hands in my jean pockets and walk a little farther until I see the basketball courts. It’s a beautiful day out, and some boys are playing a game of H.O.R.S.E. I step closer and stop at the chain link fence. I recognize a few faces from school, but they don’t look in my direction, and I don’t bother to wave. I hear one of them make a joke about another boy’s mother, and though my face wants to break into a smile, it doesn’t happen. It stays inside me, hidden but bothersome. An unscratched itch. I’m uncertain of the last time I smiled. The wave hits me out of nowhere. I truly do not see or feel any warning at all, but suddenly, I’m clutching the fence with one hand and projectile vomiting into the weeds and confetti of cigarette butts by my feet. The bits of egg and bacon are slimy with my stomach bile and the thought of how slick they must feel makes me vomit again. Tears blur my vision and I feel a line of spittle hanging from my bottom lip. I blink to clear my eyes, and watch the drool swing like a silver pendulum. The sum of my insides shake and quake and tremor. I spit into the weeds. My throat feels like I’ve spewed lava. I am a volcano. I let out a heavy sigh, use my hand to smear the spit from my lips, and then wipe my hand on my jeans. The game has come to a halt and I realize all of the boys are frozen on the court, staring at me. I cough, spit again, and head back home. My back feels hot from their eyes on me. I don’t tell my parents I threw up, but later, when they’re sleeping and the house is quiet, I creep downstairs, placing my steps against the sides of the treads so I don’t make them creak. I check beneath the kitchen sink and I can’t find the little blue bottle I saw Mom with months ago. I can’t specifically figure out why that bothers me so much, but lately I feel like my breath is slowly seeping out of me and rising to the surface of the pool water. It takes me a long while to go to sleep. # Mom wears this gray sweater jacket all the time like a cloak a statue might wear. It’s slightly too big for her, so she has the sleeve ends folded back twice. I tried it on once when she was napping. It’s stiff and coarse and uncomfortable even though she’s had it for a long time. I’m not sure why she wears it. It’s almost like it refuses to be broken in. It’s become a uniform of sorts, and when Mom comes in to bring me soup or check on me, I realize it’s not the bright white or patterned nurse scrubs, but in a way, the gray sweater is Mom’s nurse uniform. I’m lying down and I can’t help but glance at Della’s bed, untouched for months by anyone except me. Sometimes at night, I curl up on it, and even though the linens have been washed, if I squeeze Della’s pillow against my face, I can still smell her there. Faintly. It’s kind of like the whisper of a memory of what she smelled like, but it’s something. My stomach burns and my mind recites Shakespeare, that part about fire burn and cauldron bubble. I’ve lost track of how many times I’ve thrown up, but it’s enough that I’ve stayed home from school for three weeks. To date, I’ve gotten a total of three Get Well Soon cards. My mind swims with current like the wake of a mermaid’s tail. Della laughing with me. Dancing together. Doing stupid skits in our bedroom, or inventing our secret handshake. I think of the time she used a red make-up pencil to draw a pimple on her chin so I wouldn’t feel so bad about the real one I had. Della went to school like that, too. All of these things make me smile, and I realize how foreign that feeling has become. I was born six minutes and thirty-two seconds before Della. I know this because Mom says thirty-two seconds in labor feels like an hour. So technically I’m the older sister, but the thing is, I look up to Della. She has some quality I will never have, and that’s okay. Until now, we’ve been together, literally forever. Split from the same cell, sharing everything , right on back to the moment of conception. I love her. She loves me, too. I should have known. A part of me knew, but I guess I kept my eyes tightly closed rather than face something uncomfortable . I consider opening the new issue of Girls’ Life Mom put on the nightstand for me, but simply touching the glossy pages makes me think of how my vomit looked at the basketball court. Besides, flipping the pages and seeing all the fake smiles is something I don’t want to deal with. The bedroom ceiling is off-white with age, and the plaster is a pattern of half-circle swirls from some kind of brush. I try counting the rough crescent moons on the ceiling, but lose track after I hit forty-three. I start over and reach fifty-seven before I lose my spot again, and I give up. I hear them downstairs, talking as they play cards. I can’t make out their words, but occasionally, laughter punctuates the low hum of idle chatter. I blink, and the sunlight in the bedroom has changed. I wonder how long I’ve been asleep. A swirl of pain runs through my stomach and I turn on my side, pull my knees up, and hold them close, like I’m stuck mid-cannonball on the way to a splashdown. I breathe slowly, and the pain subsides. I release a long exhale, stare at the ceiling and imagine a silver chain of bubbles rising from my mouth. Turning onto my back, my arms flop to my sides and I stretch my legs out straight. Minus the nails and crown of thorns, I’m Christ-like. I hear the creak of footsteps coming upstairs and I look at the slightly open bedroom door, waiting for Mom to ease it open and bring in a tray. But Dad is standing there peering at me, one hand against the door. When he sees my eyes are open, he gives me a thin smile and walks in. Today, he’s the deliverer of food to the leper colony, population one. I stare at the curls of steam rising from the bowl on the tray he’s carrying. Dad sets it down on my nightstand, puts his hand in his pockets, and shows me that paper-thin smile again. “How you feeling, kiddo?” I shrug and look at the bowl of soup. The steam reminds me of fresh chlorine powder tossed in the pool, and how it swirls in the water jet. Dad’s gaze remains on me and I can feel him waiting for a real answer to his question, but I don’t give him one. The spoon handle feels slick from the steam, and that bothers me, but I pick it up and take a big bite of the chicken noodle soup. “Glad your appetite’s still good, at least.” I swallow another spoonful. It doesn’t taste like it’s right out of the can. It’s been seasoned, too much salt, but also tastes of something bitter beneath the broth. “Mom make this?” I look at Dad, now waiting for a real answer from him. He nods, his papercut smile widening a little. “Made it special. We both did.” We hold eye contact for a moment, and though my eyes are wide open, I get the same uncomfortable feeling as if I was under water, like I’m stuck between worlds. Dad is the first to look away, mutters softly under his breath, “Well…” which is his way of saying he’s leaving. “Daddy?” He pauses, one hand on the doorknob, and turns back to me. “Yeah, Punkin’?” I stare at him, watch his face as I intentionally swallow another spoonful of soup. His expression gives away nothing, but the lids of his eyes are red-rimmed, like they were at Della’s funeral. I want to tell him it’s alright, and I understand. I want to hug him, though I don’t remember the last time we did that. I want to pat his back and console him like I’m the parent and he’s the child. I want to plant a kiss on his cheek that tastes salty like chicken soup, but also of something bitter and feral and invasive. I want to tell him I’m tired of waiting on the bottom of the pool. Instead, I speak softly. “I bet freedom tastes sweet.” Dad’s eyebrows shift slightly but do not knit together in a worried expression. He stares wordlessly, gives me a small nod, and then eases the door almost closed. I’m not sure if what I said was for me or for him, but I take another spoonful of soup, feel the noodles worm their way down to the molten lava of my stomach. The pieces of chicken are too chewy, but I swallow them anyway, and keep going until I lift the bowl with two hands and drain it to the very last drop. Quite simply put, Gwendolyn Kiste is one of the finest fantasists working today, her stories driven not only by a powerful sense of myth, but also a keen insight into the faults and frailties of human nature. Her novellas Pretty Marys All in A Row and The Invention of Ghosts are astounding works of supernatural dark fantasy which reinvent tropes for her own purposes, a skill she puts to marvelous effect in her short story collection, And Her Smile Will Untether The Universe. The title story of said collection left me breathless. I believe she will do the same for you, now.
